title: NASA NeoWs Vocabulary
description: Controlled vocabulary of key terms used in the NASA Near Earth Object Web Service (NeoWs) API.

terms.definition: An asteroid or comet whose orbit brings it into proximity with Earth (within 1.3 astronomical units of the Sun). The primary subject of the NeoWs API dataset.
An asteroid with a minimum orbit intersection distance (MOID) of 0.05 AU or less AND an absolute magnitude (H) of 22.0 or brighter. PHAs are flagged via is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid.
Small-Body Primary Key identifier assigned by the NASA JPL Small-Body Database. Used as the primary asteroid identifier (neo_reference_id) in NeoWs.
A measure of an asteroid's intrinsic brightness. Lower H values indicate a larger or more reflective object. The field absolute_magnitude_h carries this value.
An event where an asteroid passes within a defined proximity to Earth (or another solar-system body). Each NearEarthObject record includes a close_approach_data array.
The distance between the asteroid and the orbiting body at the moment of closest approach, expressed in astronomical units, lunar distances, kilometers, and miles.
The speed of the asteroid relative to Earth at closest approach, expressed in km/s, km/h, and mph via the relative_velocity object.
An asteroid tracked by the JPL Sentry impact monitoring system as having a non-negligible chance of Earth impact within the next 100 years. Indicated by is_sentry_object.
The time in days required for an asteroid to complete one full orbit around the Sun. Stored as orbital_data.orbital_period.
Half the length of the longest axis of the elliptical orbit, expressed in astronomical units. Stored as orbital_data.semi_major_axis.
A measure of how circular or elongated an orbit is (0 = perfect circle, <1 = ellipse). Stored as orbital_data.eccentricity.
The point in an asteroid's orbit closest to the Sun. Captured in orbital_data.perihelion_distance (AU), orbital_data.perihelion_argument, and orbital_data.perihelion_time.
The point in an asteroid's orbit farthest from the Sun. Captured in orbital_data.aphelion_distance (AU).
A nearly conserved quantity used to classify small bodies by their orbital relationship with Jupiter. Stored as orbital_data.jupiter_tisserand_invariant.
The closest distance between the osculating orbits of two objects. Stored as orbital_data.minimum_orbit_intersection; critical for PHA classification.
Classification of an asteroid's orbit type (e.g., Apollo, Amor, Aten, Atira). Stored in orbital_data.orbit_class with type, description, and range sub-fields.
The mean distance from the Earth to the Sun (~149,597,870.7 km). Used as a distance unit throughout NeoWs for miss distances and orbital parameters.
The mean distance from the Earth to the Moon (~384,400 km). Used alongside AU as an intuitive miss-distance unit in close_approach_data.
A public NASA API key with reduced rate limits (30 requests/hour, 50/day) that can be used without registration for testing NeoWs endpoints.
